---
title: "2nd King's-UNICAMP Workshop: GWAS QC and PWAS tutorial"
author: "by Rodrigo R. R. Duarte (rodrigo.duarte@kcl.ac.uk)"
date: "2026-06-30"
output: html_document
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

This tutorial will guide you through installing two conda/mamba environments, where you will perform standard quality control of GWAS results, perform a proteome-wide association study (PWAS), and analyse biological processes underlying the GWAS results on a website called FUMA.  

# 1) Set up environments for processing GWAS files and performing PWAS (`fusion_final` and `ldsc`)

## Create `fusion_final` environment

FUSION is a statistical genetics tool that lets you go one step beyond a standard GWAS. A GWAS tells you that certain genetic variants (SNPs) are statistically associated with a trait or disease, but most of these SNPs sit in non-coding regions of the genome, so it is often unclear *which* gene, or *which* protein, they are actually affecting, or how. FUSION addresses this gap by using pre-computed "weights" — essentially a statistical recipe, built from a reference dataset, describing how a person's genotype at a set of SNPs predicts the expression level of a gene, or the abundance of a protein, in a particular tissue (e.g. blood, brain, or — in this tutorial — the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex from the ROSMAP project - see https://www.synapse.org/Synapse:syn23245237).

By combining these weights with your GWAS summary statistics, FUSION statistically "imputes" (i.e. predicts) how each gene's or protein's expression/abundance would relate to disease risk, without you needing to directly measure gene expression or protein levels in your own GWAS cohort. This approach is called a transcriptome-wide association study (TWAS) when applied to gene expression, or a proteome-wide association study (PWAS) when applied to protein levels — hence the name of this tutorial. The output is a list of genes or proteins whose genetically-predicted levels are positively or negatively associated with the disease or trait in your GWAS, which gives you a much more direct, biologically interpretable starting point (e.g. candidate genes/proteins for follow-up experiments, or potential drug targets) than the raw list of significant SNPs alone from the GWAS. 

FUSION depends on a fairly specific, and somewhat old, combination of R, Python and C/C++ libraries, which can clash with software you may already have installed on your computer. To avoid this, we will install everything inside an isolated software environment, managed by `conda`/`mamba`, called `fusion_final`. Think of this environment as a self-contained "sandbox": all the exact package versions FUSION needs are installed together in their own little space, completely separate from anything else on your machine, so they can't interfere with (or be broken by) other software, and can be easily and cleanly removed later if needed.

```{bash, eval=F}

# Create an pwas folder and enter it
mkdir -p ~/pwas 
cd ~/pwas

# Download yml file for conda
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rodrigoduarte88/kings_unicamp_pwas/refs/heads/main/fusion_final_environment.yml

# Create conda environment called fusion_final
mamba env create -f fusion_final_environment.yml -y

# Start environment. Note: conda and mamba are sister commands, but I typically just use mamba for installing new commands, and conda for activating them.
conda activate fusion_final

# Change name of some libraries for conda
cd $CONDA_PREFIX/lib
mv liblapack.so libRlapack.so 
mv libblas.so libRblas.so

# Then start R and manually install plink2R library. First, activate environment
R

# Then install plink2R
devtools::install_github("carbocation/plink2R/plink2R", ref="carbocation-permit-r361", upgrade = "never")

# install mystical libraries
install.packages(c("optparse", "doMC"), repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")

# quit R
quit("no")
```

## Create `ldsc` environment

LDSC (LD Score Regression) is a software suite originally developed to estimate SNP-based heritability and genetic correlations from GWAS summary statistics, by making use of linkage disequilibrium (LD) — the tendency of nearby SNPs on a chromosome to be inherited together, rather than independently. In this tutorial, we are not using LDSC's heritability analyses directly; instead, we only need one of its companion scripts, `munge_sumstats.py`, as a quality-control and reformatting tool.

GWAS summary statistics files downloaded from different consortia or websites can vary considerably in their column names, file formats, and the types of genetic variants they include (for example, some files contain very rare variants, poorly imputed SNPs, or "strand-ambiguous" SNPs whose alleles cannot be unambiguously matched across datasets — all of which can cause problems in downstream analyses if not removed). `munge_sumstats.py` standardises all of this for us: it renames columns into a consistent format, filters out problematic SNPs, and outputs a clean, "munged" version of the GWAS summary statistics that FUSION can reliably read in a later step.

Because LDSC was written for an older Python ecosystem, it has its own set of software dependencies that would conflict with those required by FUSION. We therefore install it in its own separate, isolated `conda`/`mamba` environment, called `ldsc`, for the same reasons explained above for `fusion_final`.

```{bash, eval=F}
cd ~/pwas

git clone -b ldsc39 https://github.com/CBIIT/ldsc.git
cd ldsc
mamba create --file environment3.yml 
conda activate ldsc

# Test if ldsc is working. Should print a bunch of options.
./munge_sumstats.py --help

```

# 2) Download required files and decompress

To run a PWAS, FUSION needs three different types of input file, each providing a different piece of the puzzle:

- **GWAS summary statistics**: the output of a genome-wide association study, summarising, for each SNP tested, how strongly it is statistically associated with the trait or disease of interest (here, schizophrenia). For each SNP this typically includes its effect size, p-value, the sample size it was tested in, and the alleles involved. This is the dataset we want to "explain" biologically — i.e. ask which genes or proteins the genetic risk captured in this GWAS might be acting through.

- **Reference panel**: a separate dataset of real, individual-level genotypes (here, from the 1000 Genomes Project, European-ancestry subset) used to model the patterns of linkage disequilibrium (LD) between nearby SNPs. GWAS summary statistics alone only tell you about one SNP at a time, but neighbouring SNPs are often correlated with each other. FUSION needs the reference panel to properly account for this correlation structure when combining SNP weights with the GWAS results.

- **SNP weights**: pre-computed prediction models (one per gene/protein, sometimes per tissue) that describe how genotypes at nearby SNPs predict the expression level of a gene, or the abundance of a protein. These weights are built in advance from a separate reference dataset where both genotypes *and* gene expression/protein levels were directly measured in the same individuals. This is the key ingredient that lets FUSION estimate, indirectly, how genetically-predicted gene/protein levels relate to disease risk in your own GWAS, without you ever needing to directly measure gene expression or protein abundance in the GWAS samples yourself.

```{bash, eval=F}

# Download GWAS summary statistics from the PGC website (European subset, schizophrenia)
cd ~/pwas
wget https://figshare.com/ndownloader/files/34517828 -O schizophrenia_EUR.tsv.gz

# Download reference panel (1000 Genomes, European subset)
wget https://www.dropbox.com/s/oxt4sbryxxjpg1d/1000G_ref_panel.tgz

# Download pwas weights for FUSION
wget xxx

# Download FUSION package
wget https://github.com/gusevlab/fusion_twas/archive/master.zip -O fusion.zip

# Download ldsc software
git clone https://github.com/bulik/ldsc.git 

# Decompress files
gunzip schizophrenia_EUR.tsv.gz 
tar zxvf FUSION_weights.tgz 
tar zxvf 1000G_ref_panel.tgz 
unzip fusion.zip

# Adapt munge_sumstats.py script to ask it to run python2, not "any python available"
sed -i 's|#!/usr/bin/env python|#!/usr/bin/env python2|g' ~/pwas/ldsc/munge_sumstats.py 

```

# 3) Explore and pre-process GWAS results

```{bash, eval=F}

# Explore first few lines
head schizophrenia_EUR.tsv 

# Explore first 100 lines
head -100 schizophrenia_EUR.tsv 

# Explore last few lines
tail schizophrenia_EUR.tsv

# Remove all lines starting with hashtag (#)
sed -i '/^#/ d' schizophrenia_EUR.tsv

# Check file again, to see how it looks 
head schizophrenia_EUR.tsv 

# Use munge_sumstats.py script from the ldsc pipeline to remove rare variants or other variants that offend typical QC criteria (see https://github.com/bulik/ldsc)
conda activate ldsc 

~/pwas/ldsc/munge_sumstats.py --sumstats schizophrenia_EUR.tsv --out schizophrenia.processed --snp ID --a1 A1 --a2 A2 --p PVAL --N-col NEFF --info IMPINFO --chunksize 500000

```

# 4) Run the pwas

```{bash, eval=F}
conda activate fusion_final

# Running the initial analysis
Rscript --verbose --no-save fusion_twas-master/FUSION.assoc_test.R \
--sumstats schizophrenia.processed.sumstats.gz \
--weights wrapped/CMC.pos \
--weights_dir wrapped \
--ref_ld_chr LDREF_harmonized/1000G.EUR. \
--chr 20 \
--out schizophrenia.chr20.dat

# How many features are expressed on chromosome 20?
wc -l schizophrenia.chr20.dat # 177 - header = 176 expression features

# Extract Bonferroni significant features.
cat schizophrenia.chr20.dat | awk 'NR == 1 || $NF < 0.05/176' > schizophrenia.chr20.dat.Sig

# Run the conditional analysis
Rscript fusion_twas-master/FUSION.post_process.R \
--input schizophrenia.chr20.dat.Sig \
--sumstats  schizophrenia.processed.sumstats.gz \
--ref_ld_chr LDREF_harmonized/1000G.EUR. \
--out schizophrenia.chr20.dat.Sig.PostProc.dat \
--chr 20 \
--save_loci \
--plot \
--locus_win 100000 

```

#### Analyse schizophrenia.chr22.PostProc.dat, other resulting files, and plots!
